header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: People Analytics -- Making the Numbers Talk
lesson.menu_desc: Transformar dados de RH numa história que um conselho de pesquisadores aceita: separar correlação de causa, nomear a variável de confusão antes que Prescott o faça, e entregar um insight acionável sem exagerar o que o número diz -- data storytelling, correlation, causation, confounding variable, statistical significance, sample size, outlier, margin of error, leading indicator, actionable insight, to control for, to skew, to read too much into, to tease out, to connect the dots. Estrutura: passiva de relato (it is estimated that... / turnover is thought to be linked to... / retention is reported to have improved...) + comparativos de dados (twice as likely as / a threefold increase / significantly higher than) -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 10 -- People Analytics
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Lesson 10 -- People Analytics

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Claire, the night before the council. I have seen your deck, and the finding is a good one, so let me protect it from the one mistake that will sink it in that room. You have a slide that shows engagement rising and turnover falling in the same three months, and it is tempting to stand up and say the mentorship program did that. Do not say it. The moment you turn a correlation into a cause in front of Alan Prescott, he stops listening to the rest of you, and he is right to. Here is what I have watched work. Name the confounding variable yourself, out loud, before he can. The reorganization landed in exactly those three months too, and either of them could be moving the numbers, so say so. Then do the thing that is genuinely yours, which is to tease out the one signal that survives both explanations, and to call it what it is, a leading indicator, not a proof. And keep the margin of error on the slide. I know it makes the number look smaller. It also makes you look like someone who has done this before. The people who overclaim in that room are never invited back to it.

Claire, it is Alan. You asked what would make me trust a set of HR numbers, so here it is, and you may pass it to her. I am not difficult about data. I am difficult about people who read too much into it. If she shows me two lines moving together and tells me one caused the other, we are finished in the first minute, because that is not a finding, it is a coincidence with ambition. What I am actually listening for is whether she knows what she cannot say. If she tells me the sample is small and shows me the margin of error without being asked, I relax. If she names the variable she could not control for before I raise it, I lean in. And if, after all of that honesty, she can still tease out one signal and tell me plainly what a manager should do with it on Monday, then she has done something most consultants never do in a year, which is to turn a number into a decision without lying about what the number is. That is the whole job. The rest is decoration.

reading.rtitle: What a Number Cannot Say on Its Own
paras: Every People and Culture leader eventually stands in front of a chart with two lines that move together, and feels the pull to say the sentence that will ruin the presentation: this caused that. Engagement went up, turnover went down, the mentorship program launched in between, and the story writes itself. The problem is that the story is data storytelling in the worst sense of the phrase. Two numbers moving together is a correlation, and a correlation is not yet a reason. Causation is one thing actually producing another, and a line on a slide can hint at it but never prove it.

The gap between the two is almost always a confounding variable, a hidden third factor quietly driving both numbers while you credit your own program. In this case the reorganization landed in exactly the same quarter as the mentorship launch, and it is thought to have shifted the workload of half the teams. Either event could be moving engagement, and no chart can separate them for you. The only honest move is to name the variable you could not control for out loud, before anyone in the room does it for you. It is believed, across the field, that the fastest way to lose a research audience is to skew a story toward the explanation that flatters you.

This is where the discipline lives. A single outlier, one team that tripled its scores after a change of manager, can drag an average far enough to look like a trend, so you check the sample size and you keep the margin of error on the slide even though it makes the number look smaller. Statistical significance is reported to matter less to executives than it should, precisely because it is the boring test that separates a real result from the noise of a small sample. You do not drop it to look confident. You keep it to look like someone who has done this before.

So what is left to say, once you have refused to overclaim? More than it seems. You can still tease out the one signal that survives every rival explanation, and you can call it what it is, a leading indicator rather than a proof, a thing that tends to move before the outcome does. And then you connect the dots into a single actionable insight, the sentence a manager can act on this Monday and not merely admire on a slide. The number never speaks for itself. Your job is not to make it say more than it can. Your job is to make it say the one true thing it can, clearly enough that the room can act on it.
reading.source: Field note, People & Culture -- adapted for Maple Bear Canada

gist.prompt: Read once for the main idea. Which sentence is the argument of the whole text?
choices: The mentorship program caused engagement to rise and turnover to fall, and the data proves it.
Because correlation is not causation, the honest analyst names the confounding variable and the margin of error, and then makes the number say the one true thing it can.
Statistical significance no longer matters to executives, so it can safely be dropped from the slide.
People analytics is unreliable, and HR leaders should avoid presenting numbers to researchers at all.

items: The text says that two numbers moving together is enough to prove that one caused the other.
It says the opposite: a correlation is not yet a reason, and a chart can hint at causation but never prove it.
A confounding variable is a hidden third factor that drives both numbers while you credit your own program.
That is the definition given, with the reorganization landing in the same quarter as the mentorship launch.
The writer advises keeping the margin of error on the slide even though it makes the number look smaller.
It makes you look like someone who has done this before, rather than someone overclaiming.
A single outlier is harmless because one team can never move an average.
The text says one outlier team can drag an average far enough to look like a trend, which is why you check the sample size.
Once you refuse to overclaim, the text says there is nothing useful left to present.
It says there is more than it seems: you tease out the one surviving signal and call it a leading indicator, then turn it into an actionable insight.

items.situation: "Your two lines move together. So the mentorship program raised engagement. Correct?"
tips: It is tempting to read it that way, but the reorganization landed in the same quarter, so I would call this a correlation I cannot yet attribute. What I can defend is a leading indicator, not a cause.
concede the correlation, name the confounding variable yourself, then downgrade the claim to what the data can carry

items.situation: "One team tripled its score. That is your evidence?"
tips: That team is an outlier, and on a sample this size it skews the average more than it should. Drop it and the effect shrinks but survives, which is the version I would ask you to act on.
identify the outlier, admit it skews the mean, then show the signal that survives without it

items.situation: "You did not control for tenure. New joiners always score differently."
tips: You are right, and I should have said so first. Once we control for tenure, the gap narrows but does not close, and it is that residual gap I am reporting, not the raw one.
concede the missing control before defending, then report the residual effect, not the inflated one

items.situation: "Is this statistically significant, or is it the noise of a small sample?"
tips: At this sample size the margin of error is wide, and I would not present it as significant. It is thought to be a real signal, but I am flagging it as directional and asking for a second read in March.
state the margin honestly, refuse the word significant if it is not earned, and ask for the follow-up

items.situation: "So what am I actually supposed to do with this on Monday?"
tips: One thing: treat the onboarding window as a leading indicator and watch it monthly, rather than waiting for turnover to confirm the problem a quarter too late. That is the actionable insight, and it does not depend on the causal claim.
the close: hand over one action that holds even if the causation never does

items.situation: "Every consultant I meet reads too much into a chart. Convince me you do not."
tips: By telling you what this number cannot say before I tell you what it can. It is a correlation, the sample is small, and I could not fully control for the reorganization. What survives all of that is the one thing I am asking you to act on.
lead with the limits, not the conclusion -- the honesty is the argument
